topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring intrusion in the otherwise quiet afternoon. My stomach clenched, a familiar, tight knot forming beneath my ribs. It was a profile picture I hadn't seen in years—a sneering grin that used to feel like a physical blow. Hesitantly, I tapped the request. Seeing their name pop up felt like stepping back onto shaky ground. My hands started to sweat, the heat prickling my palms. I stared at the message prompt, the words swimming unreadable. A wave of profound unease washed over me, making my breath catch in my throat. I quickly navigated away from the screen, shoving my phone into my pocket as if it were suddenly radioactive. The silence of my room suddenly felt heavy, suffocating.

[story 2]

When the alert chimed, I froze mid-sentence while trying to focus on my homework. It was him. Mark. The sheer shock made my vision swim for a second. I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving a cold, clammy residue on my skin. My fingers hovered over the screen, trembling so violently I had to consciously stop them from touching anything. A deep, hollow ache settled in my chest, a crushing weight that made it hard to draw a full breath. I wanted the floor to swallow me whole. Slowly, painstakingly, I minimized the application, the movement agonizingly slow, as if moving through thick mud. I just needed this feeling to dissipate.

[story 3]

I picked up my phone, intending only to check a text, but the sight of the friend request stopped me dead. A strange, nauseating lurch took hold of me. My jaw tightened involuntarily, and I could feel a prickling behind my eyes. Looking at that familiar, cruel avatar, a profound sense of deflation settled deep in my core. It was a familiar, unwelcome echo from a time I desperately wanted to erase. I let the phone drop onto my desk with a soft thud. I squeezed my eyes shut, focusing intensely on the pattern of the wood grain on my desk, trying to anchor myself against the sudden, overwhelming tide of distress that threatened to pull me under.
